_source: NREMT Paramedic standards, ACLS guidelines, PHTLS protocols, Emergency medicine references
_note: Long-tail EMS/paramedic knowledge. Assessment protocols, medication dosing, trauma management, cardiac rhythms, advanced airways.

scene_size_up.safety_first: BSI (body substance isolation), scene safe for crew/patient/bystanders; PPE based on mechanism
scene_size_up.mechanism_of_injury: MOI determines injury pattern; high-speed MVC = spine precautions; fall >20 ft = multi-system trauma
scene_size_up.number_of_patients: Single patient = full ALS; multiple patients = triage and call for resources (MCI if >5-10 patients)
scene_size_up.additional_resources: Fire for extrication, law enforcement for safety, helicopter for critical trauma (transport >30 min to trauma center)

primary_assessment.xabcs: eXsanguinating hemorrhage (stop massive bleeding first with tourniquet), Airway, Breathing, Circulation, Disability (neuro), Exposure
primary_assessment.general_impression: Sick or not sick?; stable vs unstable; determines urgency of interventions and transport
primary_assessment.level_of_consciousness: AVPU: Alert, Verbal, Painful, Unresponsive; or GCS (Glasgow Coma Scale 3-15)
primary_assessment.airway_assessment: Patent? Maintainable with positioning? Need airway adjunct (OPA/NPA)? Need advanced airway (intubation)?
primary_assessment.breathing_assessment: Rate (normal 12-20), depth, work of breathing, lung sounds bilateral, SpO2; assist if <10 or >30, shallow, or SpO2 <94%
primary_assessment.circulation_assessment: Pulse rate/quality, skin color/temp/condition, capillary refill <2 sec, signs of shock
primary_assessment.priority_determination: Load and go (trauma, stroke, STEMI, severe respiratory distress) vs stay and play (stable medical, minor trauma)

secondary_assessment.sample_history: Symptoms, Allergies, Medications, Past medical history, Last oral intake, Events leading up
secondary_assessment.opqrst_pain: Onset, Provocation/Palliation, Quality, Region/Radiation, Severity (0-10), Time/Timing
secondary_assessment.vital_signs: BP, pulse, respirations, SpO2, temperature, ETCO2 (if capnography available), blood glucose
secondary_assessment.head_to_toe_exam: Inspect and palpate; DCAP-BTLS (Deformities, Contusions, Abrasions, Punctures, Burns, Tenderness, Lacerations, Swelling)
secondary_assessment.focused_exam: If medical or isolated injury, focus on chief complaint area; full head-to-toe if trauma with significant MOI or altered mental status

glasgow_coma_scale.eye_opening: 4 = Spontaneous, 3 = To voice, 2 = To pain, 1 = None
glasgow_coma_scale.verbal_response: 5 = Oriented, 4 = Confused, 3 = Inappropriate words, 2 = Incomprehensible sounds, 1 = None
glasgow_coma_scale.motor_response: 6 = Obeys commands, 5 = Localizes pain, 4 = Withdraws from pain, 3 = Flexion to pain (decorticate), 2 = Extension to pain (decerebrate), 1 = None
glasgow_coma_scale.interpretation: 15 = Normal, 13-14 = Mild TBI, 9-12 = Moderate TBI, 3-8 = Severe TBI (intubate if ≤8)
glasgow_coma_scale.trending: Decreasing GCS = deteriorating neurological status; repeat every 15 min or with any change

cardiac_arrest_vfib_vtach.cpr: High-quality: 100-120 compressions/min, 2-2.4 inch depth, full recoil, minimize interruptions (<10 sec), rotate compressor every 2 min
cardiac_arrest_vfib_vtach.defibrillation: Shock as soon as possible; biphasic 120-200J (device specific); resume CPR immediately for 2 min; recheck rhythm
cardiac_arrest_vfib_vtach.medications: Epinephrine 1 mg IV/IO every 3-5 min; Amiodarone 300 mg IV/IO after 3rd shock (repeat 150 mg once)
cardiac_arrest_vfib_vtach.reversible_causes: H's and T's: Hypovolemia, Hypoxia, Hydrogen ion (acidosis), Hypo/hyperkalemia, Hypothermia, Tension pneumothorax, Tamponade (cardiac), Toxins, Thrombosis (coronary or pulmonary)
cardiac_arrest_vfib_vtach.rosc: Return of spontaneous circulation: pulse check, ETCO2 >40 mmHg sudden rise, waveform capnography

cardiac_arrest_pea_asystole.no_shock: Non-shockable rhythms; continue CPR, search for reversible causes
cardiac_arrest_pea_asystole.epinephrine: 1 mg IV/IO every 3-5 min; give as soon as access obtained
cardiac_arrest_pea_asystole.treatable_causes: H's and T's most likely; tension pneumothorax (needle decompression), hypovolemia (fluid bolus), hyperkalemia (calcium chloride)

bradycardia_with_pulse.assessment: Symptomatic? (Chest pain, SOB, altered mental status, hypotension, shock); HR typically <50 with symptoms
bradycardia_with_pulse.atropine: 0.5 mg IV every 3-5 min (max 3 mg) if symptomatic; may not work for 2nd/3rd degree block
bradycardia_with_pulse.pacing: Transcutaneous pacing if atropine ineffective or 2nd/3rd degree block; 60-80 BPM, increase mA until capture, sedate if conscious
bradycardia_with_pulse.dopamine_epinephrine: Dopamine 2-10 mcg/kg/min or Epinephrine 2-10 mcg/min infusion if pacing unavailable

tachycardia_with_pulse.stable_vs_unstable: Unstable = hypotension, acute altered mental status, chest pain, shock; stable = alert, BP adequate
tachycardia_with_pulse.unstable: Synchronized cardioversion; sedate if possible; 50-100J initial (narrow complex), 120-200J (wide complex or AFib)
tachycardia_with_pulse.stable_narrow_complex: Vagal maneuvers (Valsalva, carotid massage if no bruits); Adenosine 6 mg rapid IV push, flush, 12 mg if no conversion, 12 mg again
tachycardia_with_pulse.stable_wide_complex: Assume VTach if unknown; Amiodarone 150 mg IV over 10 min, or Lidocaine 1-1.5 mg/kg if Amiodarone unavailable; prepare for cardioversion if deteriorates

12_lead_ecg_interpretation.stemi_criteria: ST elevation >1 mm in 2 contiguous limb leads or >2 mm in 2 contiguous precordial leads; new LBBB with symptoms = STEMI equivalent

stemi_localizations.inferior: Leads II, III, aVF; RCA occlusion; do right-sided ECG (V4R) to check RV involvement; avoid nitro if RV infarct
stemi_localizations.anterior: Leads V1-V4; LAD occlusion; large infarct, high mortality; pump failure risk
stemi_localizations.lateral: Leads I, aVL, V5-V6; LCX occlusion; often with inferior or anterior
stemi_localizations.posterior: Tall R wave in V1-V2, ST depression V1-V3; do posterior leads V7-V9; LCX occlusion

12_lead_ecg_interpretation.pre_hospital_stemi_care: Aspirin 324 mg chew, IV access, O2 if SpO2 <94%, Nitro 0.4 mg SL (if BP >100 systolic, no RV infarct), 12-lead transmission to hospital, direct to cath lab
12_lead_ecg_interpretation.time_goals: Door-to-balloon <90 min; first medical contact to balloon <120 min; prehospital 12-lead reduces time by 15-30 min

basic_airways.head_tilt_chin_lift: Non-trauma; opens airway by lifting tongue off posterior pharynx
basic_airways.jaw_thrust: Suspected C-spine injury; maintains neutral alignment; difficult to maintain for extended period
basic_airways.opa_oropharyngeal: Unconscious patients without gag reflex; size = corner of mouth to earlobe; insert upside-down then rotate 180° (or use tongue blade)
basic_airways.npa_nasopharyngeal: Semi-conscious with gag reflex; size = tip of nose to earlobe; lubricate, insert bevel toward septum; contraindicated in basilar skull fracture

supraglottic_airways.king_lt: Dual-cuff; insert until depth marker at teeth; inflate both cuffs with single syringe; ventilate through tube
supraglottic_airways.igel: Gel cuff conforms to anatomy; no inflation needed; insert until depth marker at teeth; easier insertion than King
supraglottic_airways.lma: Laryngeal mask airway; inflatable cuff; visualize vocal cords with glidescope if available; allows higher ventilation pressures than King/iGel
supraglottic_airways.advantages: Faster than intubation; less training; effective for BVM-difficult patients; rescue airway if intubation fails
supraglottic_airways.limitations: No protection from aspiration; limited ventilation pressures (20-25 cmH2O max); can dislodge during transport

endotracheal_intubation.indications: Apneic, unable to protect airway (GCS ≤8), prolonged ventilation needed, failure of other methods
endotracheal_intubation.equipment: Laryngoscope (Mac 3-4 or Miller 2-3 blade), ETT (7.0-8.0 mm adult), stylet, 10cc syringe, ETCO2, suction ready
procedure: 3. RSI if conscious: Premedicate (Fentanyl, Lidocaine optional), Induction (Etomidate 0.3 mg/kg or Ketamine 1-2 mg/kg), Paralytic (Succinylcholine 1-2 mg/kg or Rocuronium 1 mg/kg)
4. Wait for paralysis (Sux 30-60 sec, Roc 60-90 sec); apply cricoid pressure if assistant available
5. Laryngoscopy: Mac blade in vallecula, Miller blade under epiglottis; lift handle up and forward (don't lever on teeth)
6. Visualize cords, pass tube through cords, inflate cuff 5-10cc air
7. Confirm: 5-point check: 1-Visualization, 2-Bilateral breath sounds, 3-No epigastric sounds, 4-Fogging in tube, 5-ETCO2 waveform 35-45 mmHg
8. Secure tube, note depth at teeth (21-23 cm adult male, 19-21 cm female), reconfirm after every move
endotracheal_intubation.difficult_airway_predictors: LEMON: Look (facial trauma, small mouth), Evaluate 3-3-2 (3 fingers mouth opening, 3 fingers mentum to hyoid, 2 fingers hyoid to thyroid), Mallampati (tongue size), Obstruction (epiglottitis, angioedema), Neck mobility
endotracheal_intubation.failed_airway: Cannot intubate, cannot oxygenate; immediate surgical airway (cricothyrotomy) if BVM and supraglottic fail

cricothyrotomy.indications: Cannot intubate, cannot ventilate; massive facial trauma; airway obstruction above cords (angioedema, epiglottitis, foreign body)
cricothyrotomy.landmark: Cricothyroid membrane; between thyroid cartilage (Adam's apple) and cricoid cartilage; horizontal palpable space
cricothyrotomy.procedure: Stabilize larynx; vertical skin incision; horizontal membrane incision; insert tracheal hook to stabilize, dilate with forceps; insert 6.0 cuffed ETT or Shiley; confirm placement
cricothyrotomy.commercial_kits: Quicktrach, Melker (wire-guided Seldinger technique); faster but higher failure rate than open surgical
cricothyrotomy.complications: Bleeding, false passage, posterior tracheal wall perforation, subcutaneous emphysema; definitive airway at hospital

hemorrhage_control.direct_pressure: First step; 3-5 min continuous pressure; upgrade if ineffective
hemorrhage_control.tourniquet: Extremity hemorrhage not controlled by pressure; high and tight (2-3 inches above wound); tighten until bleeding stops; note time; do not remove in field
hemorrhage_control.hemostatic_gauze: Combat Gauze, Celox, ChitoGauze; pack deep into wound (Z-fold technique); hold pressure 3 min; junctional hemorrhage (groin, axilla, neck)
hemorrhage_control.blood_products: Whole blood or 1:1:1 ratio (RBC:Plasma:Platelets) preferred over crystalloid; massive transfusion protocol; limit crystalloid to <1L to avoid dilutional coagulopathy

shock_types.hypovolemic: Blood or fluid loss; tachycardia, hypotension, cool skin, delayed cap refill; treat with blood products (trauma) or fluids (dehydration)
shock_types.cardiogenic: Pump failure (MI, CHF); tachycardia, hypotension, pulmonary edema, JVD; treat cause (STEMI to cath lab), avoid fluids
shock_types.distributive: Septic (infection), anaphylactic (allergen), neurogenic (spinal cord injury); vasodilation; warm skin (septic), hypotension; treat with fluids + vasopressors (septic), epinephrine (anaphylaxis), fluids + vasopressors (neurogenic)
shock_types.obstructive: Tension pneumothorax (needle decompression), cardiac tamponade (pericardiocentesis), pulmonary embolism (anticoagulation at hospital)

tension_pneumothorax.signs: Decreased breath sounds one side, hyperresonance to percussion, tracheal deviation (late), JVD, hypotension, respiratory distress
tension_pneumothorax.treatment: Needle decompression: 14g or 16g IV catheter, 2nd intercostal space, midclavicular line (or 5th ICS anterior axillary line if obese), just over top of rib (avoid neurovascular bundle below rib); rush of air confirms; tape catheter, monitor
tension_pneumothorax.chest_tube: Definitive treatment at hospital; needle decompression is temporary field fix

chest_trauma.flail_chest: ≥3 ribs fractured in ≥2 places; paradoxical movement (segment moves in on inspiration); underlying pulmonary contusion common; positive pressure ventilation stabilizes; pain control
chest_trauma.open_pneumothorax: Sucking chest wound; 3-sided occlusive dressing (allows air out, not in); monitor for tension (if develops, burp dressing)
chest_trauma.hemothorax: Blood in pleural space; decreased breath sounds, dullness to percussion; IV access, blood products, rapid transport; chest tube at hospital

head_trauma.cushing_triad: Late sign of increased ICP: Hypertension, Bradycardia, Irregular respirations; indicates herniation imminent
head_trauma.management: Maintain CPP (Cerebral Perfusion Pressure) = MAP - ICP; keep MAP >80; avoid hypotension and hypoxia (secondary brain injury); HOB 30° if no spinal injury; maintain ETCO2 35-40 (avoid hyperventilation unless signs of herniation)
head_trauma.hyperventilation: Only if signs of herniation (blown pupil, posturing, Cushing's triad); target ETCO2 30-35; causes vasoconstriction reducing ICP but also reducing cerebral blood flow
head_trauma.mannitol_hypertonic_saline: Hospital treatments for increased ICP; not typically given prehospital; Mannitol 0.25-1 g/kg, 3% saline 250cc bolus

spinal_immobilization.indications: Trauma with concerning MOI (MVC >40 mph, fall >3 ft, axial load, diving); midline spine pain/tenderness; altered mental status; neurological deficits; distracting injury
spinal_immobilization.nexus_criteria: Can clinically clear C-spine if all 5 present: No posterior midline tenderness, No focal neuro deficits, Normal alertness, No intoxication, No painful distracting injury
spinal_immobilization.technique: Manual stabilization → collar → backboard (or vacuum mattress) → secure torso then head; maintain neutral alignment; pad voids; reassess neurovascular after immobilization

epinephrine.anaphylaxis: 0.3-0.5 mg (1:1,000) IM lateral thigh; repeat every 5-15 min if needed
epinephrine.bradycardia: 2-10 mcg/min IV infusion (add 1 mg to 500cc = 2 mcg/mL)

vasopressors.dopamine: 2-20 mcg/kg/min IV infusion; 2-5 = renal, 5-10 = cardiac, 10-20 = vasopressor; dose-dependent receptor activity; second-line to epinephrine or norepinephrine
vasopressors.norepinephrine: 0.1-0.5 mcg/kg/min; potent alpha (vasoconstriction); used for septic shock; not typically carried prehospital

antiarrhythmics.amiodarone: Cardiac arrest VF/VTach: 300 mg IV/IO, repeat 150 mg once; Stable VTach: 150 mg IV over 10 min, repeat PRN to max 2.2 g/24hr; Side effects: hypotension (slow rate), bradycardia
antiarrhythmics.lidocaine: Cardiac arrest VF/VTach (if Amiodarone unavailable): 1-1.5 mg/kg IV/IO, repeat 0.5-0.75 mg/kg every 5-10 min to max 3 mg/kg; Stable VTach: 1-1.5 mg/kg, then 0.5-0.75 mg/kg every 5-10 min; Side effects: CNS toxicity (seizures, confusion) at high doses
antiarrhythmics.adenosine: SVT: 6 mg rapid IV push, flush immediately, 12 mg if no effect, 12 mg again; Half-life 10 sec; causes brief asystole (warn patient); contraindicated in WPW with AFib (can cause VFib)

induction_agents.etomidate: 0.3 mg/kg IV; sedative-hypnotic; hemodynamically neutral (safe in shock); short-acting (5-10 min); no analgesia; adrenal suppression with repeated doses
induction_agents.ketamine: 1-2 mg/kg IV; dissociative; maintains airway reflexes better than others; bronchodilation (good for asthma); increases ICP (avoid in head injury per old protocols, controversial); causes emergence delirium (give benzodiazepine)
induction_agents.propofol: 1-2 mg/kg IV; rapid onset; causes hypotension (avoid in shock); burns on injection; white liquid (Diprivan)

paralytics.succinylcholine: 1-2 mg/kg IV; depolarizing; rapid onset 30-60 sec; short duration 5-10 min; fasciculations; contraindicated in hyperkalemia, burns >24 hr old, crush injury, neuromuscular disease (causes hyperkalemia); malignant hyperthermia risk (rare)
paralytics.rocuronium: 1 mg/kg IV (can use 1.2 mg/kg for faster onset); non-depolarizing; onset 60-90 sec; duration 30-60 min; safe in hyperkalemia; no fasciculations; reversible with Sugammadex (not widely available prehospital)

adjuncts.fentanyl: 1-3 mcg/kg IV; opioid analgesic; blunts sympathetic response to laryngoscopy; short-acting; respiratory depression
adjuncts.lidocaine: 1.5 mg/kg IV; blunts ICP rise from laryngoscopy (theoretical); given 2-3 min before induction if head injury

naloxone.indications: Opioid overdose; respiratory depression <10/min or apnea; altered mental status
naloxone.dosing: 0.4-2 mg IN/IV/IM/IO; can repeat every 2-3 min to max 10 mg; titrate to respirations >10, not full consciousness (avoid precipitated withdrawal)
naloxone.intranasal: Narcan spray 4 mg per nostril; easy, safe, bystander-friendly; absorbed through nasal mucosa
naloxone.duration: 30-90 min; shorter than most opioids; patient can re-sedate; transport all overdoses, observe 2+ hours

stroke.cincinnati_stroke_scale: Facial droop, Arm drift, Speech slurred; 1 finding = 72% stroke probability
stroke.last_known_well: Critical for tPA eligibility (within 3-4.5 hours); if wake-up stroke, use time last seen normal
stroke.pre_hospital_care: Rapid assessment, FAST exam, blood glucose check (hypoglycemia mimics stroke), large-bore IV, direct to stroke center, pre-notification
stroke.stroke_scales: LAMS (Los Angeles Motor Scale), RACE, VAN (Vision, Aphasia, Neglect); screen for large vessel occlusion (LVO) which needs thrombectomy
stroke.contraindications_to_note: Recent surgery, bleeding disorder, seizure at onset (may not be stroke); hospital will decide tPA eligibility

sepsis.qsofa: Quick SOFA: Altered mental status, Respiratory rate ≥22, Systolic BP ≤100; 2 of 3 = high sepsis risk
sepsis.lactate: If available prehospital; >2 mmol/L concerning, >4 severe
sepsis.treatment: Large-bore IV access × 2, fluid bolus 30 mL/kg (≈2 liters adult) crystalloid, blood cultures before antibiotics (if hospital delay), pre-notification for early antibiotics
sepsis.source_control: Identify source (pneumonia, UTI, skin infection, abdominal); hospital needs to treat source plus antibiotics

toxidromes.opioids: Pinpoint pupils, respiratory depression, altered mental status; treat with naloxone
toxidromes.sympathomimetics: Cocaine, meth; tachycardia, hypertension, hyperthermia, agitation, dilated pupils; cool, sedate (benzos), no beta-blockers
toxidromes.anticholinergics: Antihistamines, jimson weed; 'mad as a hatter, blind as a bat, red as a beet, hot as a hare, dry as a bone'; treat supportively, benzos for agitation
toxidromes.cholinergics: Organophosphates (pesticides), nerve agents; SLUDGE: Salivation, Lacrimation, Urination, Defecation, GI distress, Emesis; also bradycardia, bronchospasm; treat with Atropine 2-5 mg IV (double every 5 min until secretions dry), 2-PAM if nerve agent

obstetric_emergencies.imminent_delivery: Crowning, uncontrollable urge to push, contractions <2 min apart; prepare for field delivery
obstetric_emergencies.delivery_procedure: Support head, check for nuchal cord (slip over head or clamp and cut if tight), deliver anterior shoulder then posterior, deliver body, dry and stimulate baby, clamp cord (2 clamps 6 inches apart, cut between), deliver placenta (don't pull, let it deliver naturally), fundal massage
obstetric_emergencies.apgar_score: At 1 and 5 min: Appearance, Pulse, Grimace, Activity, Respiration; 0-2 points each; 7-10 = normal, 4-6 = moderate distress, 0-3 = severe distress
obstetric_emergencies.neonatal_resuscitation: Dry, warm, stimulate; suction if needed; BVM if no respiratory effort after stimulation; compressions if HR <60 after 30 sec PPV; compressions 3:1 ratio with ventilations (90 compressions + 30 breaths = 120 events/min)
obstetric_emergencies.postpartum_hemorrhage: Fundal massage, oxytocin 10 units IM (if available), position supine, large-bore IV, blood products if available, rapid transport
